items.prompt_md: In the longest-increasing-subsequence table, what does `dp[i]` represent?
options: The length of the longest increasing subsequence that ends exactly at index i
The length of the longest increasing subsequence using only the first i elements
The number of increasing subsequences that end at index i
The value of nums[i] itself
items.answer: The length of the longest increasing subsequence that ends exactly at index i
items.explanation_md: dp[i] is anchored to index i as the last element of the subsequence, which is why the final answer is max(dp) rather than dp[n-1].

items.prompt_md: Why does computing dp[i] require scanning every earlier index j < i, instead of just checking j = i - 1?
options: Because the subsequence that extends best into i could end at any earlier j, not just the immediate neighbor
Because nums is not sorted, so a single lookback would miss array bounds
Because the recurrence must average over all previous values
Because dp[i-1] is always undefined until the full table is built
items.answer: Because the subsequence that extends best into i could end at any earlier j, not just the immediate neighbor
items.explanation_md: Unlike a fixed one-step lookback, an increasing subsequence can skip freely, so any earlier j with nums[j] < nums[i] is a valid predecessor to check.

items.prompt_md: Why is the final answer max(dp) over the whole table, rather than dp[n-1]?
options: Because the longest increasing subsequence overall might not end at the last index
Because dp[n-1] is never computed correctly
Because the array might contain duplicate values
Because dp values must be summed, not maxed
items.answer: Because the longest increasing subsequence overall might not end at the last index
items.explanation_md: dp[i] only measures subsequences ending at i, so the best one overall could end anywhere; scanning the whole table finds it.

items.prompt_md: What condition must hold before dp[i] can be updated from dp[j] in the LIS recurrence?
options: nums[j] < nums[i]
dp[j] > dp[i]
nums[j] == nums[i]
items.answer: nums[j] < nums[i]
items.explanation_md: Only a strictly smaller earlier value can be legally extended by nums[i] while keeping the subsequence increasing.

items.prompt_md: Why does the O(n^2) LIS solution initialize every dp[i] to 1 before the loops run?
options: Because every single element, taken alone, is an increasing subsequence of length 1
Because the array is guaranteed to be sorted
Because dp[0] must always equal the array length
Because 1 is the identity element for addition in the final max
items.answer: Because every single element, taken alone, is an increasing subsequence of length 1
items.explanation_md: The base case for any index is that it forms a trivial subsequence by itself before any extension is found.

items.prompt_md: How does the word-break decision problem (can the string be split into dictionary words?) map onto the same 'scan every earlier point' shape as LIS?
options: reachable[i] is true if some earlier reachable[j] is true and the substring from j to i is a dictionary word, checked over every j < i
reachable[i] is true only if reachable[i-1] is true and the last character matches a word
reachable[i] is computed by sorting the dictionary words by length first
reachable[i] is always true unless the string is empty
items.answer: reachable[i] is true if some earlier reachable[j] is true and the substring from j to i is a dictionary word, checked over every j < i
items.explanation_md: Like LIS, the valid predecessor for position i could be any earlier split point j, not a fixed lookback, so every j < i must be tried.

items.prompt_md: In word-break, what does the boolean array `reachable[i]` mean?
options: Whether the prefix of the string ending at index i can be fully segmented into dictionary words
Whether the character at index i is the start of a word
Whether index i is inside exactly one dictionary word
The number of ways to segment the prefix ending at i
items.answer: Whether the prefix of the string ending at index i can be fully segmented into dictionary words
items.explanation_md: reachable[i] tracks segmentability of the prefix up to i, mirroring dp[i] in LIS tracking the best subsequence ending at i.

items.prompt_md: What is the key difference between LIS's dp[i] and word-break's reachable[i], even though both scan all earlier indices?
options: LIS's transition is keyed by an ordering condition (nums[j] < nums[i]); word-break's is keyed by a reachability/membership condition (is the substring a dictionary word)
LIS uses booleans while word-break uses integers
LIS scans forward only, word-break scans backward only
There is no difference; they solve the identical problem
items.answer: LIS's transition is keyed by an ordering condition (nums[j] < nums[i]); word-break's is keyed by a reachability/membership condition (is the substring a dictionary word)
items.explanation_md: Both share the 'check every earlier index' shape, but what makes a predecessor valid differs: a value comparison versus a dictionary lookup.

items.prompt_md: For longest common subsequence (LCS) between two strings of length m and n, what does dp[i][j] typically represent?
options: The length of the longest common subsequence between the first i characters of one string and the first j characters of the other
Whether the two strings are identical up to index i
The number of characters that differ between the two strings
The longest increasing subsequence of the concatenated string
items.answer: The length of the longest common subsequence between the first i characters of one string and the first j characters of the other
items.explanation_md: LCS extends the 'subsequence, order preserved, gaps allowed' idea to two sequences at once, requiring a 2-D table indexed by position in each string.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the O(n^2) tabulated longest-increasing-subsequence solution shown in the lesson?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
items.explanation_md: For each of the n indices, the inner loop scans all earlier indices, giving n * n = O(n^2) time and O(n) space for the table.

items.prompt_md: The lesson mentions a patience-sorting variant of LIS using binary search over the smallest tail value for each subsequence length. What time complexity does that variant achieve?
options: O(n log n)
O(log n)
O(n)
items.answer: O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: Maintaining one tail array and binary-searching into it for each of the n elements gives n binary searches of O(log n) each.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the O(n^2) LIS tabulation as written (excluding the input array)?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: The dp array holds one entry per index, so it uses O(n) space even though the time cost is quadratic.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the brute-force approach that checks every possible subsequence for the increasing property?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
items.explanation_md: There are 2^n possible subsequences of an n-element array, and checking each one is what the dp table avoids.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of integers and need the length of the longest subsequence that is strictly increasing, where the subsequence need not be contiguous.
options: 1-D DP where dp[i] is the best subsequence length ending at i, checking every earlier index with a smaller value
Two pointers, one starting at each end of the array
Sort the array and binary search for each element's position
Greedily keep extending as long as the next element is larger than the current one
items.answer: 1-D DP where dp[i] is the best subsequence length ending at i, checking every earlier index with a smaller value
items.explanation_md: The trigger is 'best subsequence, order preserved, gaps allowed, extend on a comparison against any earlier element' — a full scan of all earlier dp states is required since a greedy single pass misses non-adjacent improvements.

items.prompt_md: Given a string and a dictionary of words, determine whether the string can be segmented into a sequence of one or more dictionary words.
options: 1-D DP where reachable[i] is true if some earlier reachable[j] is true and substring j..i is a dictionary word
Use two pointers scanning from both ends of the string toward the middle
Sort the dictionary words alphabetically and binary search for prefixes
Use a stack to match opening and closing word boundaries
items.answer: 1-D DP where reachable[i] is true if some earlier reachable[j] is true and substring j..i is a dictionary word
items.explanation_md: The valid split point for position i could be any earlier index j, matching the 'scan every earlier state' shape of subsequence DP rather than a fixed-window check.

items.prompt_md: You have two strings and need the length of their longest common subsequence (characters in the same relative order in both, gaps allowed in either).
options: 2-D DP over positions in both strings, since a single index can't capture progress through two sequences at once
1-D DP over just the first string's length
Two pointers advancing through both strings simultaneously
Greedily match the next equal character in both strings
items.answer: 2-D DP over positions in both strings, since a single index can't capture progress through two sequences at once
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: extending the subsequence idea to two sequences needs two indices worth of state (a 2-D table), not the plain 1-D table used for single-sequence LIS or word-break; it still belongs to the same DP family as this lesson, but the state has grown a dimension.

items.prompt_md: An array is already sorted in strictly increasing order, and you need to find whether a target value exists in it.
options: Binary search, since the sorted order lets you eliminate half the remaining range each step
1-D DP where dp[i] tracks the best subsequence ending at i
Backtracking over every possible subset
Track a running max and running min while scanning
items.answer: Binary search, since the sorted order lets you eliminate half the remaining range each step
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: the array being sorted and the question being existence-of-a-value (not longest-subsequence) means binary search is the right tool, not the LIS-style DP from this lesson.

items.prompt_md: You must find the length of the longest chain of pairs (a, b) where each next pair's first element is strictly greater than the previous pair's second element, chain order following the input order but allowing skips.
options: 1-D DP where dp[i] is the longest chain ending at pair i, checking every earlier pair for compatibility
Sort the pairs and greedily take every pair that fits
Use a stack to track compatible pairs
Two pointers over the sorted list of pairs
items.answer: 1-D DP where dp[i] is the longest chain ending at pair i, checking every earlier pair for compatibility
items.explanation_md: This mirrors LIS exactly: 'best chain ending at i, extend when a compatibility condition holds against any earlier element' is the subsequence-DP trigger.

items.prompt_md: Given a small array (n <= 12), you're asked to output every subsequence that happens to be strictly increasing, not just report the longest one's length.
options: Backtracking to enumerate every subsequence directly, since the tiny bound makes exponential exploration acceptable and you need the subsequences themselves, not just a length
1-D DP where dp[i] is the best subsequence length ending at i
Binary search over subsequence lengths
Track a running max and running min while scanning
items.answer: Backtracking to enumerate every subsequence directly, since the tiny bound makes exponential exploration acceptable and you need the subsequences themselves, not just a length
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: when the problem asks for every matching subsequence (not just the longest length) under a tiny bound, brute-force backtracking is the right tool instead of the length-only DP table.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of box dimensions, find the maximum number of boxes that can be nested inside each other, where box A can nest inside box B only if every dimension of A is strictly smaller than the corresponding dimension of B, and the boxes have been pre-sorted into a valid nesting direction.
options: 1-D DP where dp[i] is the longest nesting chain ending at box i, checking every earlier box for the strictly-smaller condition
Two pointers over the sorted box list
Track a running max and running min of box volume
Backtracking over every subset of boxes
items.answer: 1-D DP where dp[i] is the longest nesting chain ending at box i, checking every earlier box for the strictly-smaller condition
items.explanation_md: Once order matches a valid nesting direction, this is exactly the LIS shape: extend dp[i] from any earlier index j where box j nests inside box i.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of integers, find the length of the longest subsequence where consecutive elements alternate between strictly increasing and strictly decreasing.
options: 1-D DP where dp[i] tracks the best 'up' and 'down' alternating lengths ending at i, checking every earlier index
Two pointers scanning from both ends
Binary search over the array
Greedily extend whenever the next element differs in direction from the last
items.answer: 1-D DP where dp[i] tracks the best 'up' and 'down' alternating lengths ending at i, checking every earlier index
items.explanation_md: This still fits the 'best subsequence ending at i, extend by scanning earlier compatible states' shape of subsequence DP, just with an alternating comparison instead of a strictly-increasing one.

items.prompt_md: You need to count the total NUMBER of distinct increasing subsequences of a given array, not just the length of the longest one.
options: 1-D DP where dp[i] accumulates the count of increasing subsequences ending at i, summing over every valid earlier index
Backtracking to enumerate every subsequence individually regardless of array size
Binary search combined with a running max
Two pointers tracking start and end of the current subsequence
items.answer: 1-D DP where dp[i] accumulates the count of increasing subsequences ending at i, summing over every valid earlier index
items.explanation_md: Swapping 'longest length' for 'total count' keeps the same scan-every-earlier-index shape — dp[i] just sums counts from compatible predecessors instead of taking a max.

items.prompt_md: Given a very large sorted array with no duplicates, determine the index of a specific target value, or report that it doesn't exist.
options: Binary search, since the sorted order lets each comparison eliminate half the remaining range
1-D DP scanning every earlier index for a compatibility condition
Track a running max and running min while scanning
Backtracking over every possible subsequence
items.answer: Binary search, since the sorted order lets each comparison eliminate half the remaining range
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: a single sorted array plus an existence-of-a-value query is a binary-search problem, unrelated to the longest-subsequence DP this lesson teaches.
